**Scenario Analysis:**
• Changes MULTIPLE variables together
• Shows range of outcomes

**Simulation (Monte Carlo):**
• Uses PROBABILITY distributions
• Runs thousands of scenarios
• Produces probability of outcomes

**Exam Tip:** Sensitivity analysis = one variable at a time!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Sensitivity analysis tests how changes in inputs affect outputs
Change ONE variable at a time to isolate its impact
Identify which assumptions have biggest effect on results
Scenario analysis changes multiple variables together
Tornado diagrams visualize relative sensitivity
Focus risk management on highly sensitive variables
Different from simulation which uses probability distributions

knowledgeCheck.question: A project's NPV is most affected by changes in sales volume and least affected by changes in the discount rate. When constructing a tornado diagram, which variable would appear at the TOP of the chart?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: In a tornado diagram, variables with the greatest impact on the output appear at the top. Sales volume, having the most effect on NPV, would be the top bar (widest). Discount rate would appear lower on the chart.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Tornado diagrams order variables from most sensitive (top) to least sensitive (bottom).

title: Marginal Analysis and Incremental Decisions
description: Use marginal costs and revenues to make optimal short-term decisions

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Every business decision should focus on what CHANGES! Marginal analysis isolates incremental costs and revenues to determine the financial impact of choosing one alternative over another. This is the foundation of relevant cost accounting.

sections.title: Marginal Analysis Fundamentals
sections.content:
**Core Principle:** Compare only the costs and revenues that DIFFER between alternatives

**Key Terms:**
• **Marginal Cost:** Additional cost of one more unit
• **Marginal Revenue:** Additional revenue from one more unit
• **Incremental Cost:** Total additional cost of a decision
• **Incremental Revenue:** Total additional revenue from a decision

**Decision Rule:**
If Incremental Revenue > Incremental Cost → Accept
If Incremental Revenue < Incremental Cost → Reject

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Relevant Costs
sections.content:
**"FORD" Rule for Relevant Costs:**

**F**uture (not past/sunk)
**O**pportunity (what are we giving up?)
**R**elevant to decision (differs between alternatives)
**D**ifferential (changes with the choice)

**If it doesn't differ, ignore it!**

sections.title: Special Order Decision
sections.content:
**Scenario:** Customer offers lower price for large order

**Analysis:**
• Compare incremental revenue to incremental cost
• Usually incremental cost = variable costs only
• Fixed costs typically don't change

**Example:**
Special order: 1,000 units at $45 each
Variable cost: $30 per unit
Allocated fixed cost: $20 per unit

**Wrong analysis:** $45 - $50 = ($5) loss per unit
**Right analysis:** $45 - $30 = $15 contribution per unit

**Accept if:**
1. Capacity available
2. Won't affect regular sales
3. Incremental revenue > incremental cost

sections.title: Make or Buy Decision
sections.content:
**Scenario:** Should we make a component or buy it?

**Relevant Make Costs:**
• Variable manufacturing costs
• Avoidable fixed costs
• Opportunity cost of space/capacity

**Irrelevant:**
• Unavoidable fixed overhead
• Allocated costs that won't go away

**Example:**
Make: Variable $8 + Avoidable fixed $2 = $10
Buy: Purchase price $11

**Decision:** Make saves $1 per unit

**But consider:** What else could we do with capacity?
If freed capacity earns $50,000 and we need 40,000 units:
Opportunity cost = $50,000 / 40,000 = $1.25 per unit
New comparison: Make $10 + $1.25 = $11.25 vs Buy $11
**Now buy is better!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Sunk Costs
sections.content:
**Never include sunk costs!**

**Common Sunk Costs:**
• Cost of equipment already purchased
• R&D already spent
• Training already completed
• Book value of existing assets

**Exam Example:**
"Machine originally cost $100,000, now has book value of $40,000"

**Trap:** Students include $40,000 in decision
**Reality:** The $100,000 (and $40,000 book value) is GONE - it's irrelevant to future decisions!

**Only current market/salvage value matters (what we could GET for it)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Marginal analysis compares only costs/revenues that differ
Relevant costs are future and differential
Sunk costs are never relevant - they're already spent
Opportunity costs are relevant - what are we giving up?
Special orders: focus on variable costs if capacity available
Make vs buy: include avoidable fixed costs and opportunity costs
Allocated fixed overhead is usually NOT relevant

knowledgeCheck.question: A company is considering a special order. The product's full cost is $50 (variable $35, fixed $15). The special order price is $42. No additional fixed costs would be incurred. What is the change in profit per unit if the order is accepted?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Incremental revenue is $42 per unit. Incremental cost is only the $35 variable cost (fixed costs don't change). Contribution = $42 - $35 = $7 increase in profit per unit. The $15 fixed cost is not relevant because it won't change.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Only variable costs are relevant when fixed costs won't change.

title: Break-Even Analysis Extensions
description: Apply CVP to multi-product companies, target profit, and margin of safety analysis

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Most companies sell multiple products with different margins! CMAs must extend basic break-even analysis to handle product mix, target profit scenarios, and risk assessment through margin of safety. These are HEAVILY TESTED topics!

sections.title: Basic Break-Even Review
sections.content:
**Single Product:**

**Break-Even Units = Fixed Costs / CM per Unit**

**Break-Even Sales $ = Fixed Costs / CM Ratio**

**Where:**
• CM per Unit = Price - Variable Cost per Unit
• CM Ratio = CM per Unit / Price

**Example:**
Price = $100, VC = $60, FC = $200,000
CM = $40, CM Ratio = 40%

BE Units = $200,000 / $40 = 5,000 units
BE Sales = $200,000 / 0.40 = $500,000

sections.title: Multi-Product Break-Even
sections.content:
**Requires weighted-average CM:**

**Step 1:** Determine sales mix (units or revenue)
**Step 2:** Calculate weighted-average CM or CM ratio
**Step 3:** Apply standard break-even formula

**Example:**
Product A: $50 CM, 60% of sales mix
Product B: $30 CM, 40% of sales mix

Weighted CM = ($50 × 0.60) + ($30 × 0.40)
Weighted CM = $30 + $12 = $42

If FC = $210,000:
BE = $210,000 / $42 = 5,000 total units
• Product A: 5,000 × 60% = 3,000 units
• Product B: 5,000 × 40% = 2,000 units

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Target Profit
sections.content:
**Think of target profit as "extra fixed costs to cover":**

**Units for Target Profit = (FC + Target Profit) / CM**

**Why it works:**
• First cover fixed costs (break-even)
• Then earn target profit
• Each unit contributes CM toward both

**For after-tax target:**
Pre-tax needed = Target / (1 - Tax Rate)

sections.title: Target Profit Analysis
sections.content:
**Before-Tax Target:**

Units = (FC + Target Profit) / CM per Unit

**After-Tax Target:**

Pre-tax Profit Needed = After-tax Target / (1 - Tax Rate)

**Example:**
FC = $200,000, CM = $40
Target after-tax profit = $75,000
Tax rate = 25%

Pre-tax needed = $75,000 / (1 - 0.25) = $100,000

Units = ($200,000 + $100,000) / $40 = 7,500 units

**Check:**
Revenue: 7,500 × $100 = $750,000
Variable: 7,500 × $60 = $450,000
CM: $300,000
Fixed: $200,000
Pre-tax profit: $100,000
Tax (25%): $25,000
After-tax: $75,000 ✓

sections.title: Margin of Safety
sections.content:
**Definition:** How far sales can drop before reaching break-even

**Formulas:**

Margin of Safety (units) = Current Sales - BE Sales

Margin of Safety (%) = (Current Sales - BE Sales) / Current Sales

**Example:**
Current sales: 8,000 units
Break-even: 5,000 units

MoS = 8,000 - 5,000 = 3,000 units
MoS % = 3,000 / 8,000 = 37.5%

**Interpretation:**
Sales can drop 37.5% before company loses money
Higher MoS = Lower risk
Lower MoS = Higher risk

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Sales Mix Changes
sections.content:
**Multi-product break-even assumes CONSTANT sales mix!**

**If mix changes:**
• Weighted-average CM changes
• Break-even point changes
• Must recalculate

**Example Impact:**
Shift from high-margin to low-margin product → Higher BE
Shift from low-margin to high-margin product → Lower BE

**Exam often tests:**
1. Calculate current weighted CM
2. Change the mix
3. Calculate new weighted CM
4. Calculate new break-even

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Multi-product requires weighted-average contribution margin
Weight CM by sales mix percentage
Target profit: add target to fixed costs before dividing by CM
After-tax target: gross up for taxes first
Margin of safety = Current sales - Break-even sales
Higher MoS % means lower risk of losses
Sales mix changes require recalculation of break-even

knowledgeCheck.question: Company sells Product X (CM $20, 70% of mix) and Product Y (CM $50, 30% of mix). Fixed costs are $145,000. What is the break-even in total units?
knowledgeCheck.hint: Calculate weighted-average CM first, then apply standard BE formula.

title: Linear Programming for Resource Optimization
description: Use constrained optimization to maximize profit or minimize cost with limited resources

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: When resources are limited, how do you maximize profit? Linear programming systematically finds the optimal product mix given constraints. CMAs use LP to make data-driven resource allocation decisions rather than guesswork.

sections.title: Linear Programming Basics
sections.content:
**Components of an LP Problem:**

**1. Decision Variables**
• What we're deciding (usually quantities)
• Example: Units of Product A and Product B

**2. Objective Function**
• What we're maximizing or minimizing
• Usually: Max Profit or Min Cost
• Example: Max Z = $40A + $50B

**3. Constraints**
• Resource limitations
• Example: 2A + 3B ≤ 100 (labor hours)

**4. Non-negativity**
• Variables ≥ 0
• Can't produce negative units

sections.title: Example Problem
sections.content:
**Scenario:**
• Product X: CM = $30, requires 2 machine hours, 3 labor hours
• Product Y: CM = $40, requires 4 machine hours, 2 labor hours
• Available: 100 machine hours, 90 labor hours

**Formulation:**

**Maximize:** Z = 30X + 40Y

**Subject to:**
• 2X + 4Y ≤ 100 (machine hours)
• 3X + 2Y ≤ 90 (labor hours)
• X, Y ≥ 0

**Goal:** Find values of X and Y that maximize Z

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: The Simplex Rule
sections.content:
**For graphical LP (2 variables), optimal solution is at a CORNER!**

**Steps:**
1. Graph all constraint lines
2. Identify feasible region (where all constraints satisfied)
3. Find corner points of feasible region
4. Evaluate objective function at each corner
5. Pick the corner with best value

**Why corners?** Linear functions are optimized at vertices, not interior points.

sections.title: Solving the Example
sections.content:
**Find corner points:**

**Point 1:** X=0, Y=0 → Z = $0

**Point 2:** X=0, Y=25 (from machine constraint)
• Z = 30(0) + 40(25) = $1,000

**Point 3:** X=30, Y=0 (from labor constraint)
• Z = 30(30) + 40(0) = $900

**Point 4:** Intersection of constraints
• 2X + 4Y = 100 and 3X + 2Y = 90
• Solving: X = 20, Y = 15
• Z = 30(20) + 40(15) = $1,200

**Optimal Solution:** X = 20, Y = 15
**Maximum Profit:** $1,200

sections.title: Sensitivity Analysis in LP
sections.content:
**Shadow Price (Dual Value):**
• Value of ONE additional unit of constraint
• How much would profit increase if you had one more machine hour?

**Allowable Increase/Decrease:**
• Range where optimal solution doesn't change
• CM can change within this range

**Binding Constraints:**
• At their maximum/minimum
• These limit the solution
• Have positive shadow prices

**Non-binding (Slack):**
• Not fully used
• Shadow price = 0 (more wouldn't help)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: When LP Works
sections.content:
**LP requires LINEAR relationships:**

✓ **Works when:**
• Constant CM per unit
• Constant resource usage per unit
• Relationships can be expressed as linear equations

✗ **Doesn't work when:**
• Economies of scale (cost per unit decreases with volume)
• Learning curves (labor decreases with experience)
• Quantity discounts (prices change with volume)

**Exam Note:** If problem has non-linear elements, LP is not appropriate!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: LP maximizes/minimizes objective function subject to constraints
Decision variables, objective function, constraints, non-negativity
Optimal solution is at a corner point of feasible region
Shadow price = value of one additional unit of constraint
Binding constraints limit the solution (shadow price > 0)
Slack in constraints means extra capacity (shadow price = 0)
Only works with linear relationships

knowledgeCheck.question: In an LP problem, the machine hours constraint has a shadow price of $8. What does this mean?
options: Each product requires $8 of machine time
One additional machine hour would increase profit by $8
The company is using 8 machine hours
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Shadow price (dual value) represents the incremental value of one additional unit of a constrained resource. An $8 shadow price means adding one more machine hour would increase profit by $8 (within the allowable range).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Shadow price measures how much the objective function improves with one more unit of the constraint.

title: Operating Leverage and Risk
description: Understand how cost structure affects profit volatility and business risk

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Cost structure is a strategic choice with risk implications! High fixed costs amplify both profits and losses. CMAs analyze operating leverage to help management understand the risk/reward tradeoff of different cost structures.

sections.title: Operating Leverage Defined
sections.content:
**Definition:** The degree to which a firm uses fixed costs in its cost structure

**High Operating Leverage:**
• High proportion of fixed costs
• Low variable costs per unit
• Examples: Airlines, software companies, factories

**Low Operating Leverage:**
• Low fixed costs
• High variable costs per unit
• Examples: Consulting firms, retailers

**Key Insight:**
High leverage = High risk AND high reward
Small sales changes cause large profit swings

sections.title: Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL)
sections.content:
**Formula:**

**DOL = Contribution Margin / Operating Income**

**Alternative Formula:**

**DOL = % Change in Operating Income / % Change in Sales**

**Example:**
Sales: $500,000
Variable costs: $300,000
Contribution margin: $200,000
Fixed costs: $150,000
Operating income: $50,000

**DOL = $200,000 / $50,000 = 4.0**

**Interpretation:**
A 10% increase in sales will cause a 40% increase in operating income!

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Leverage Sensitivity
sections.content:
**"Closer to breakeven = Higher leverage"**

**Why?** When operating income is small, DOL is high:
DOL = CM / OI

Small denominator = Big ratio!

**At breakeven:** DOL approaches infinity
**Far from breakeven:** DOL approaches 1

**Practical meaning:**
Companies near breakeven see dramatic profit swings from small sales changes

sections.title: Strategic Implications
sections.content:
**When to choose HIGH operating leverage:**
• Confident in stable/growing demand
• Want maximum profit from volume
• Industry requires automation
• Can sustain fixed costs in downturns

**When to choose LOW operating leverage:**
• Demand is uncertain/volatile
• Want flexibility to scale down
• Prefer steady, modest profits
• Can't sustain losses in downturns

**Examples:**
• Automation (high fixed) vs Outsourcing (variable)
• Own fleet (high fixed) vs Contract carriers (variable)
• Salaried staff (fixed) vs Temps (variable)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: DOL Changes
sections.content:
**DOL is not constant - it changes with sales level!**

**As sales increase above breakeven:**
• Operating income grows
• DOL decreases
• Profit becomes more stable

**As sales decrease toward breakeven:**
• Operating income shrinks
• DOL increases
• Profit becomes more volatile

**Exam Tip:** Calculate DOL at the SPECIFIC sales level given.
Don't assume it's constant!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Operating leverage = proportion of fixed costs in cost structure
DOL = Contribution Margin / Operating Income
High DOL means profit is sensitive to sales changes
High fixed costs = higher breakeven, higher risk, higher reward
DOL is highest near breakeven point
Cost structure is a strategic choice with risk implications
Match cost structure to demand stability and risk tolerance

knowledgeCheck.question: Company A has contribution margin of $300,000 and operating income of $60,000. If sales increase by 15%, what is the expected percentage change in operating income?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: DOL = $300,000 / $60,000 = 5.0. Expected change in OI = DOL × % Change in Sales = 5.0 × 15% = 75%. A 15% sales increase causes a 75% increase in operating income.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Calculate DOL first, then multiply by the percentage change in sales.

title: Make-or-Buy and Outsourcing Decisions
description: Apply relevant cost analysis to insourcing vs outsourcing decisions

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Make or buy is one of the most common strategic decisions! CMAs provide the analysis, but the answer isn't just about cost. Qualitative factors like quality control, flexibility, and core competencies often determine the right choice.

sections.title: The Make-or-Buy Decision
sections.content:
**Core Question:**
Should we produce in-house or purchase from supplier?

**Relevant Costs Only:**
• Costs that DIFFER between alternatives
• Future costs (not sunk)
• Avoidable costs only

**Typically Relevant:**
• Direct materials
• Direct labor
• Variable overhead
• Purchase price
• Incremental fixed costs

**Typically NOT Relevant:**
• Allocated fixed overhead (continues regardless)
• Depreciation on existing equipment
• Sunk costs

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Relevant Cost Analysis
sections.content:
**"DAFO" - What's relevant:**

**D**ifferential - Changes between alternatives

**A**voidable - Goes away if we don't make

**F**uture - Not already spent

**O**pportunity - What we give up

**Key Question:**
"If we buy instead of make, which costs DISAPPEAR?"

Only those disappearing costs are relevant to the decision!

**Common Trap:**
Allocated overhead that STAYS regardless = NOT relevant

sections.title: Make-or-Buy Example
sections.content:
**Current cost to make (10,000 units):**
• Direct materials: $50,000
• Direct labor: $80,000
• Variable overhead: $30,000
• Fixed overhead (avoidable): $20,000
• Fixed overhead (allocated): $40,000
• Total: $220,000 ($22/unit)

**Supplier offers: $18/unit = $180,000**

**Analysis:**
| Cost | Make | Buy |
|------|------|-----|
| Direct materials | $50,000 | - |
| Direct labor | $80,000 | - |
| Variable OH | $30,000 | - |
| Avoidable fixed | $20,000 | - |
| Purchase | - | $180,000 |
| **Total Relevant** | **$180,000** | **$180,000** |

**Indifferent on cost - Consider qualitative factors!**

sections.title: Qualitative Factors
sections.content:
**Favor Make (In-House):**
• Protect proprietary technology
• Ensure quality control
• Maintain supply security
• Utilize existing capacity
• Preserve jobs and morale

**Favor Buy (Outsource):**
• Focus on core competencies
• Access supplier expertise
• Reduce capital investment
• Increase flexibility
• Share risk with supplier

**Always Consider:**
• Supplier reliability
• Long-term cost trends
• Reversibility of decision
• Impact on workforce

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Opportunity Costs
sections.content:
**When capacity has alternative use:**

**Include opportunity cost in "Make" column**

**Example:**
If we make Part A, we can't use capacity for Product B
Product B contribution = $30,000

**Make cost now includes:**
• Variable costs: $160,000
• Avoidable fixed: $20,000
• Opportunity cost: $30,000
• **Total: $210,000**

**Buy: $180,000**
→ Buy is now preferred ($30,000 savings)

**Key:** Opportunity cost only applies if you have
a profitable alternative use for the capacity!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Include only relevant costs: Differential, Avoidable, Future, Opportunity
Allocated overhead that continues regardless is NOT relevant
Opportunity cost: Include if capacity has profitable alternative use
Qualitative factors often override quantitative analysis
Make favors: Quality control, proprietary technology, supply security
Buy favors: Core competency focus, flexibility, supplier expertise
Consider long-term implications, not just current costs

knowledgeCheck.question: A company makes 5,000 components at: DM $10, DL $8, VOH $4, Allocated FOH $6. An outside supplier offers $23/unit. $2/unit of the fixed overhead is avoidable. What is the relevant cost per unit to MAKE?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Relevant costs: DM $10 + DL $8 + VOH $4 + Avoidable Fixed $2 = $24. The $4 allocated but unavoidable fixed overhead is NOT relevant since it continues regardless of the decision.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Only include costs that would DISAPPEAR if you switch to buying.

title: NPV, IRR, and Capital Budgeting
description: Evaluate long-term investments using discounted cash flow techniques

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Capital budgeting determines how companies invest for the future! CMAs analyze major investments using NPV, IRR, and other techniques. These methods are heavily tested on the CMA exam - master the calculations AND know when each method works best.

sections.title: Capital Budgeting Methods Overview
sections.content:
**Discounted Cash Flow Methods (Best):**
• NPV - Net Present Value
• IRR - Internal Rate of Return
• MIRR - Modified IRR
• Profitability Index

**Non-Discounted Methods (Simpler):**
• Payback Period
• Discounted Payback
• Accounting Rate of Return (ARR)

**Key Principle:**
All methods should use CASH FLOWS, not accounting income
• Operating cash flows
• Initial investment
• Working capital changes
• Terminal value / salvage

sections.title: NPV and IRR Compared
rows: Sum of PV of cash flows - Initial investment
Assumes reinvest at cost of capital

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: NPV Calculation Steps
sections.content:
**"ICFT" - What goes into NPV:**

**I**nitial investment (negative, at t=0)
• Include installation, training, etc.

**C**ash flows from operations
• After-tax operating cash flows
• Add back depreciation

**F**ixed capital recovery (salvage)
• PV of ending salvage value
• Tax on gain/loss on sale

**T**erminal working capital release
• WC invested at start comes back at end

**NPV = PV(All Future CFs) - Initial Investment**

sections.title: NPV Example
sections.content:
**Investment:**
• Cost: $100,000
• Annual cash flow: $30,000 for 5 years
• Salvage value: $10,000
• Required return: 10%

**Calculation:**
PV of annual CFs = $30,000 × PVIFA(10%, 5)
= $30,000 × 3.7908 = $113,724

PV of salvage = $10,000 × PVIF(10%, 5)
= $10,000 × 0.6209 = $6,209

Total PV = $113,724 + $6,209 = $119,933

**NPV = $119,933 - $100,000 = $19,933**

Decision: Accept (NPV > 0)
Project creates $19,933 of value

sections.title: IRR Limitations
sections.content:
**Problem 1: Multiple IRRs**
• Non-conventional cash flows (sign changes > 1)
• Can have multiple rates where NPV = 0
• Solution: Use MIRR or NPV

**Problem 2: Scale Differences**
• Small project may have higher IRR
• But large project creates more value
• Solution: Consider NPV for ranking

**Problem 3: Reinvestment Assumption**
• IRR assumes reinvest at IRR rate
• Unrealistic for high-IRR projects
• Solution: MIRR uses more realistic rate

**Bottom Line:**
NPV is theoretically superior
But IRR is popular ("we need 15% return")

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Payback Period
sections.content:
**Payback = Time to recover initial investment**

**Limitations (Know for exam!):**
• Ignores time value of money (unless discounted)
• Ignores cash flows after payback
• Arbitrary acceptance criterion

**Why still used?**
• Simple and intuitive
• Measures liquidity risk
• Useful for screening

**Discounted Payback:**
• Better - uses PV of cash flows
• Still ignores post-payback cash flows

**If asked which is theoretically best:**
NPV is ALWAYS the answer!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: NPV: Accept if positive; Best method theoretically
IRR: Accept if > required return; Has limitations with non-conventional flows
Use cash flows, not accounting income
Include initial investment, operating CFs, salvage, working capital
NPV assumes reinvestment at cost of capital (realistic)
IRR assumes reinvestment at IRR (often unrealistic)
Payback ignores time value and post-payback flows

knowledgeCheck.question: Project A: NPV = $50,000, IRR = 18%. Project B: NPV = $80,000, IRR = 15%. Cost of capital is 10%. If you can only choose one, which is better and why?
options: Project A - Higher IRR indicates better efficiency
Project B - Higher NPV means more value created
Both are acceptable - either choice is correct
Need more information about project size
knowledgeCheck.explanation: NPV is the superior method for ranking mutually exclusive projects because it measures absolute value creation. Project B creates $80,000 of value vs. $50,000 for A. IRR measures efficiency but not scale - A might be smaller.
knowledgeCheck.hint: When projects are mutually exclusive, which method better captures total value creation?

title: Pricing Strategies and Methods
description: Set prices using cost-based, market-based, and value-based approaches

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Pricing directly impacts profitability and market position! CMAs provide the cost data and analysis essential for pricing decisions. Understanding different pricing approaches helps you support strategic pricing across the organization.

sections.title: Pricing Approaches
sections.content:
**Cost-Based Pricing:**
• Start with costs, add markup
• Ensures cost recovery
• May ignore market conditions

**Market-Based Pricing:**
• Start with competitor prices
• Position relative to market
• May not cover costs

**Value-Based Pricing:**
• Start with customer value
• Price reflects perceived value
• Requires understanding customer

**Target Costing:**
• Start with market price
• Back into allowable cost
• Design to cost target

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Target Costing Process
sections.content:
**"PDCE" - Target Costing Steps:**

**P**rice - What will market pay?
• Research competitive prices
• Assess customer willingness

**D**educt - Required profit margin
• Target Price - Target Profit = Allowable Cost

**C**ompare - Current cost estimate
• Gap = Current estimate - Allowable cost
• This is cost reduction target

**E**ngineer - Design to cost
• Value engineering
• Design alternatives
• Supplier negotiations

**Example:**
Market price: $100
Required profit: $15
Allowable cost: $85
Current estimate: $95
Cost reduction target: $10

sections.title: Pricing Strategies
sections.content:
**Penetration Pricing:**
• Low price to gain market share
• Build volume, scale
• Risk: May never reach profitability

**Skimming:**
• High initial price
• Extract value from early adopters
• Lower price over time

**Premium Pricing:**
• High price signals quality
• Luxury, prestige positioning

**Loss Leader:**
• Below-cost pricing
• Drive traffic for other sales

**Dynamic Pricing:**
• Prices vary with demand
• Airlines, hotels, ride-sharing

sections.title: Special Pricing Situations
sections.content:
**Special Orders:**
• One-time order, excess capacity
• Accept if price > incremental cost
• Consider opportunity cost

**Product Line Pricing:**
• Price products relative to each other
• Good/Better/Best tiers
• Complementary products

**Bundling:**
• Package products together
• Increase total sale
• May reduce perceived unit price

**Transfer Pricing:**
• Internal transactions
• Cost, market, or negotiated
• Tax implications

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Price Elasticity
sections.content:
**Elasticity affects pricing decisions!**

**Price Elasticity of Demand:**
= % Change in Quantity / % Change in Price

**Elastic (>1):**
• Price increase → Big demand drop
• Many substitutes
• Commodities
• Lower prices may increase revenue

**Inelastic (<1):**
• Price increase → Small demand drop
• Few substitutes
• Necessities, loyal customers
• Can raise prices

**Unit Elastic (=1):**
• Revenue constant regardless of price

**Know: Elastic products need competitive prices; Inelastic allows premium pricing

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Cost-based: Start with costs + markup; ensures recovery
Market-based: Start with competitor prices
Value-based: Start with customer perceived value
Target costing: Market price - Profit = Allowable cost
Penetration: Low price for market share
Skimming: High initial price, reduce over time
Elastic demand: Price changes cause big volume changes
Special orders: Accept if price > incremental cost

knowledgeCheck.question: Market research shows customers will pay $80 for a new product. The company requires a 20% profit margin on selling price. What is the target cost?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Target profit = $80 × 20% = $16. Target cost = $80 - $16 = $64. The engineering team must design the product to cost no more than $64 to achieve the required margin. If current cost estimate exceeds $64, value engineering is needed.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Calculate the required profit first, then subtract from the market price.

title: Revenue Analysis and Forecasting
description: Analyze revenue drivers and build accurate revenue forecasts

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Revenue drives everything else! The revenue forecast cascades into production plans, inventory levels, staffing, and capital needs. CMAs must understand revenue drivers and forecasting methods to support effective planning.

sections.title: Revenue Decomposition
sections.content:
**Revenue = Price × Volume**

**Price Analysis:**
• List price vs. realized price
• Discount patterns
• Mix effects
• Price changes over time

**Volume Analysis:**
• Unit sales trends
• Customer count
• Transactions per customer
• Market share changes

**Revenue Variance:**
• Price variance: (Actual - Budgeted price) × Actual volume
• Volume variance: (Actual - Budgeted volume) × Budgeted price
• Mix variance: Effect of product/customer mix

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Revenue Driver Tree
sections.content:
**Break down revenue into components:**

**For B2C:**
Revenue = Customers × Visits × Conversion × Transaction Size

**For B2B:**
Revenue = Accounts × Products/Account × Units/Product × Price

**For Subscription:**
Revenue = Subscribers × Revenue/Sub × (1 - Churn)

**For Services:**
Revenue = Consultants × Utilization × Bill Rate

**Why decompose?**
• Identify which lever moved
• Set targets for each driver
• Improve forecast accuracy

sections.title: Time Series Components
sections.content:
**Trend (T):**
• Long-term direction
• Growth or decline
• Linear, exponential, etc.

**Seasonality (S):**
• Regular, predictable patterns
• Monthly, quarterly, annual
• Adjust for comparison

**Cyclical (C):**
• Economic cycles
• Multi-year patterns
• Harder to predict

**Irregular (I):**
• Random variation
• Unexplained noise
• One-time events

**Y = T × S × C × I (multiplicative)
Y = T + S + C + I (additive)

sections.title: Forecast Accuracy
sections.content:
**Error Measures:**
• Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)
• Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE)
• Root Mean Square Error (RMSE)

**MAPE = Average(|Actual - Forecast| / Actual)
• < 10% = Excellent
• 10-20% = Good
• 20-50% = Reasonable
• > 50% = Poor

**Bias Detection:**
• Consistently over or under?
• Sales sandbagging?
• Optimistic management?

**Tracking Signal:**
= Cumulative Error / MAD
• Should stay within ±4
• Outside = systematic bias

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Forecast Assumptions
sections.content:
**Document and test assumptions!**

**Common Assumption Errors:**
• Past growth continues
• Market share stable
• No new competitors
• Economy unchanged
• Pricing power maintained

**Scenario Analysis:**
• Best case / Base case / Worst case
• Vary key assumptions
• Understand sensitivity

**Sanity Checks:**
• Is growth realistic?
• Is market big enough?
• Where does share come from?
• What resources needed?

**Hockey stick forecasts should be challenged!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Revenue = Price × Volume; decompose both
Methods: Sales force, time series, regression, market-based
Time series: Trend, Seasonality, Cyclical, Irregular
MAPE < 10% = excellent forecast accuracy
Track bias - over/under forecasting patterns
Document assumptions and test sensitivities
Break revenue into driver trees for better analysis

knowledgeCheck.question: Budgeted: 10,000 units at $50. Actual: 9,000 units at $55. What is the favorable price variance?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Price Variance = (Actual Price - Budgeted Price) × Actual Volume = ($55 - $50) × 9,000 = $5 × 9,000 = $45,000 favorable. We earned $5 more per unit than budgeted on the 9,000 units actually sold.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Price variance uses actual volume. Calculate the per-unit difference times units sold.

title: Capacity Analysis
description: Understand and optimize production capacity utilization

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Capacity drives profitability and cost! CMAs analyze capacity utilization to find improvement opportunities, cost excess capacity accurately, and support capital investment decisions. Understanding capacity is essential for pricing and make-or-buy analysis.

sections.title: Types of Capacity
sections.content:
**Theoretical Capacity:**
• Maximum possible output
• 24/7/365 operation
• No downtime
• Rarely achieved

**Practical Capacity:**
• Theoretical minus normal downtime
• Maintenance, setups, breaks
• Realistically achievable
• Used for overhead rates

**Normal Capacity:**
• Average expected over time
• Considers demand cycles
• Traditional denominator

**Budgeted Capacity:**
• Expected for current period
• Based on sales forecast
• May vary widely

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Capacity Costing
sections.content:
**Why Capacity Choice Matters:**

**Using Normal Capacity:**
• Spreads costs over 'normal' volume
• Smooths product costs
• May hide capacity costs

**Using Practical Capacity:**
• Product cost = only used capacity
• Unused capacity = period expense
• Shows true cost of idle capacity
• GAAP and IFRS preferred

**Example:**
• Fixed overhead: $1,000,000
• Practical capacity: 100,000 units
• Actual production: 80,000 units

Practical approach:
• Rate: $10/unit
• Applied: $800,000
• Idle capacity cost: $200,000 (expense)

sections.title: Bottleneck Analysis
sections.content:
**Constraint/Bottleneck:**
The resource limiting total output

**Identifying Bottlenecks:**
• Highest utilization
• Work-in-process buildup
• Downstream waiting

**Theory of Constraints (TOC):**
• Focus on bottleneck
• Maximize throughput at constraint
• Non-bottleneck efficiency is secondary

**Bottleneck Decisions:**
• Contribution margin per constraint unit
• Not total contribution
• Maximize scarce resource use

**Example:** If machine time is constraint:
Product A: $20 CM, takes 2 hours = $10/hour
Product B: $15 CM, takes 1 hour = $15/hour
→ Prioritize Product B!

sections.title: Capacity Decisions
sections.content:
**When Capacity Constrained:**
• Prioritize high-margin products
• Consider outsourcing
• Evaluate capacity expansion
• Manage customer allocations

**When Excess Capacity:**
• Accept lower-margin work
• Special orders above variable cost
• Consider capacity reduction
• Seek new markets/products

**Make vs Buy with Capacity:**
• If constrained: Outsource to free capacity
• If excess: Use capacity even if slightly higher cost
• Factor in opportunity cost

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Per-Unit vs Per-Constraint
sections.content:
**When constrained, optimize the CONSTRAINT!**

**Wrong Approach:**
Rank products by contribution margin per unit
• Ignores different constraint consumption

**Right Approach:**
Rank by contribution margin per constraint unit
• Per machine hour, per labor hour
• Whatever is the bottleneck

**Example:**
Constraint: 100 machine hours available
• Product A: $50 CM, 5 hours each = $10/hour
• Product B: $30 CM, 2 hours each = $15/hour

Produce B first! 50 units × $30 = $1,500
Then A if any capacity left

**Always ask: What's the binding constraint?

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Capacity types: Theoretical > Practical > Normal > Budgeted
Practical capacity: Used for overhead rates (GAAP preferred)
Idle capacity: Should be reported as period cost, not in product
Bottleneck: The constraint limiting total output
Optimize contribution per constraint unit, not per product
When constrained: Prioritize high-margin per constraint
When excess: Accept work above variable cost

knowledgeCheck.question: Machine time is the constraint. Product X: $60 CM, uses 4 machine hours. Product Y: $40 CM, uses 2 machine hours. Which should be produced first?
options: Product X - higher total contribution margin
Product Y - higher contribution per machine hour
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Product X: $60 / 4 hours = $15/machine hour. Product Y: $40 / 2 hours = $20/machine hour. Produce Y first because it generates more profit per unit of the constrained resource. When constraint is saturated, switch to X.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Calculate contribution margin divided by constraint consumption.

title: Sensitivity and Scenario Analysis
description: Evaluate decisions under uncertainty using sensitivity and scenarios

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: The future is uncertain - smart analysis accounts for it! CMAs use sensitivity and scenario analysis to understand how changes in assumptions impact decisions. This helps identify key risks, focus attention on what matters, and make more robust decisions.

sections.title: Sensitivity Analysis
sections.content:
**Definition:**
Measure how output changes when ONE input changes

**Process:**
1. Build base case model
2. Change one variable at a time
3. Observe impact on output
4. Identify most sensitive variables

**Output Formats:**
• Sensitivity tables
• Tornado diagrams
• Spider charts

**Key Question:**
'What if X changes by 10%?'

**Use:**
• Identify key value drivers
• Focus due diligence
• Prioritize risk mitigation

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Sensitivity Steps
sections.content:
**Building Sensitivity Analysis:**

**1. Identify Key Variables:**
• Price, volume, costs
• Growth rates, margins
• Discount rate, timing

**2. Define Range:**
• ±10%, ±20% from base
• Or specific values (optimistic/pessimistic)

**3. Calculate Impact:**
• Change ONE variable
• Hold others at base
• Record new output

**4. Rank Variables:**
• Widest swing = most sensitive
• Focus attention here

**Tornado shows:**
• Bars sorted by impact
• Widest at top = most important

sections.title: Scenario Analysis
sections.content:
**Definition:**
Evaluate multiple variables changing together

**Common Scenarios:**
• **Best case:** Optimistic assumptions
• **Base case:** Most likely assumptions
• **Worst case:** Pessimistic assumptions

**More Sophisticated:**
• Economic scenarios (recession, growth, stable)
• Competitive scenarios
• Regulatory scenarios

**Advantages over Sensitivity:**
• Variables often move together
• More realistic combinations
• Tests correlated risks

**Output:**
Decision metrics under each scenario

sections.title: Monte Carlo Simulation
sections.content:
**Advanced Approach:**
• Define probability distributions for inputs
• Run thousands of iterations
• Generate probability distribution of outputs

**Output:**
• Expected value
• Standard deviation
• Confidence intervals
• Probability of achieving target

**Example Output:**
'There is a 70% probability that NPV will exceed $0'

**When to Use:**
• Complex models
• Many uncertain variables
• Need probability estimates
• High-stakes decisions

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Sensitivity Limitations
sections.content:
**Sensitivity Analysis Limitations:**

• **One-at-a-time:** Ignores interaction effects
• **Linear:** May miss non-linear relationships
• **Arbitrary ranges:** +/-10% may not be realistic
• **No probabilities:** Doesn't say which is likely

**Scenario Analysis Limitations:**

• **Subjective:** Scenario selection is judgment
• **Limited:** Only a few scenarios tested
• **May miss:** Black swan events

**Best Practice:**
Use both! Sensitivity to find key drivers, scenario to test realistic combinations

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Sensitivity: Change ONE variable, measure impact
Tornado diagram: Rank variables by impact (widest bar = most sensitive)
Scenario: Change MULTIPLE variables together
Scenarios: Best, base, worst case - or specific situations
Monte Carlo: Probability distributions, thousands of runs
Use sensitivity to identify key drivers
Use scenarios to test realistic combinations

knowledgeCheck.question: A capital project's NPV is most sensitive to sales volume among all variables tested. What does this imply for management?
options: Sales volume forecasts deserve extra scrutiny and monitoring
The discount rate should be increased
knowledgeCheck.explanation: When NPV is most sensitive to a variable, small changes in that variable have the largest impact on project value. This means sales volume estimates deserve extra due diligence, and volume should be closely monitored post-implementation. It doesn't necessarily mean rejection.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Most sensitive variable = deserves most attention in validation.

title: Project Risk Analysis
description: Assess and manage risks in capital investment projects

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Not all projects have equal risk! CMAs must assess project-specific risks and adjust analysis accordingly. A project's NPV means nothing without understanding its risk profile. Risk-adjusted capital budgeting leads to better investment decisions.

sections.title: Sources of Project Risk
sections.content:
**Stand-Alone Risk:**
• Project viewed in isolation
• Variability of project's own returns

**Corporate Risk:**
• Project's impact on firm risk
• Correlation with existing operations
• Diversification effects

**Market Risk (Beta):**
• Project's systematic risk
• Relevant for shareholders
• Can't diversify away

**Sources:**
• Economic conditions
• Competition
• Technology changes
• Input costs
• Regulatory changes
• Execution risk

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Risk Adjustments
sections.content:
**Two Approaches to Adjust for Risk:**

**1. Risk-Adjusted Discount Rate (RADR):**
• Add premium for riskier projects
• Higher risk = higher discount rate
• Example: WACC + 3% for high-risk

**Pros:** Simple, intuitive
**Cons:** Arbitrary premium, compounds over time

**2. Certainty Equivalent (CE):**
• Convert risky cash flows to certain equivalents
• Use risk-free rate to discount
• CE factor × Expected CF

**Pros:** Theoretically correct
**Cons:** Hard to estimate CE factors

**Typical Practice:**
RADR more commonly used

sections.title: Real Options
sections.content:
**Definition:**
Managerial flexibility to adapt to future outcomes

**Types of Real Options:**
• **Option to Expand:** Scale up if successful
• **Option to Abandon:** Exit if unsuccessful
• **Option to Delay:** Wait for more info
• **Option to Contract:** Scale down
• **Option to Switch:** Change inputs/outputs

**Value:**
• Traditional NPV ignores flexibility
• Real options add value!
• Strategic NPV = NPV + Option value

**When Valuable:**
• High uncertainty
• Significant flexibility
• Time before commitment

sections.title: Decision Trees
sections.content:
**Purpose:**
Analyze sequential decisions under uncertainty

**Components:**
• **Decision nodes:** Choose action
• **Chance nodes:** Uncertain outcomes
• **End nodes:** Payoffs

**Process:**
1. Draw tree left to right
2. Assign probabilities to chance nodes
3. Work backward (roll back)
4. Calculate expected values
5. Choose highest EV path

**Example:**
• Pilot project: $1M
• Success (60%): Full project NPV $5M
• Failure (40%): Stop, lose $1M
• Expected value: 0.6 × $5M - 0.4 × $1M - $1M = $1.6M

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: WACC vs Project Rate
sections.content:
**Don't use WACC for all projects!**

**WACC is right when:**
• Project has same risk as firm average
• Same capital structure
• Same industry

**Adjust when:**
• Project is riskier/safer than average
• Different industry
• Different financing

**Example:**
Utility company (low risk, 8% WACC)
Considers tech venture (high risk)
• Using 8%: Project looks attractive
• Using 15%: Project looks bad
• Proper analysis: Use 15%!

**Project-specific beta can be estimated from comparable firms

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Project risk: Stand-alone, corporate, market (beta)
Techniques: Sensitivity, scenario, simulation, decision trees
RADR: Add risk premium to discount rate (simpler)
Certainty equivalent: Convert to certain cash flows (theoretically better)
Real options: Value of managerial flexibility (expand, abandon, delay)
Strategic NPV = Traditional NPV + Option value
Don't use WACC for projects with different risk than firm average

knowledgeCheck.question: A company can invest now or delay one year to learn market conditions. This flexibility to postpone the decision represents which type of real option?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The option to delay (or defer) allows the company to wait and gather more information before committing resources. This is valuable when there's significant uncertainty that will be resolved over time. Traditional NPV assumes immediate investment.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Which option involves timing flexibility?

title: Cost of Capital Calculation
description: Calculate component costs and WACC

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Cost of capital is THE hurdle rate for investment decisions! CMAs must calculate WACC for NPV analysis, evaluate capital structure decisions, and assess value creation. Understanding these calculations is essential for strategic financial analysis. Highly tested on CMA Part 2!

sections.title: Cost of Capital Components
sections.content:
**Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC):**
• Weighted average of all financing costs
• Reflects company's capital structure
• Used as discount rate for NPV

**Components:**
• **Cost of Debt (rD):** Interest on borrowings
• **Cost of Preferred Stock (rP):** Preferred dividends
• **Cost of Equity (rE):** Required return by shareholders

**Key Point:**
Debt is cheapest (tax shield + less risk for lenders)
Equity is most expensive (residual claim, most risk)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: CAPM
sections.content:
**Capital Asset Pricing Model:**

**rE = Rf + β(Rm - Rf)**

Where:
• **Rf:** Risk-free rate (Treasury bonds)
• **β:** Beta (stock's volatility vs market)
• **Rm:** Expected market return
• **(Rm - Rf):** Market risk premium

**Example:**
• Rf = 3%
• β = 1.2
• Rm = 10%
• Market premium = 10% - 3% = 7%

**rE = 3% + 1.2(7%) = 3% + 8.4% = 11.4%**

**Beta Interpretation:**
• β = 1.0: Same as market
• β > 1.0: More volatile than market
• β < 1.0: Less volatile than market

sections.title: WACC Calculation Example
sections.content:
**Given:**
• Capital structure: 40% debt, 60% equity
• Cost of debt: 8%
• Tax rate: 25%
• Cost of equity: 14%

**WACC:**
= (40% × 8% × (1 - 25%)) + (60% × 14%)
= (40% × 8% × 0.75) + (60% × 14%)
= 2.4% + 8.4%
= **10.8%**

**Interpretation:**
• Minimum required return on investments
• Projects earning > 10.8% create value
• Projects earning < 10.8% destroy value

sections.title: Alternative Cost of Equity Methods
sections.content:
**Dividend Growth Model (Gordon):**
rE = (D1 / P0) + g
• D1: Next year's dividend
• P0: Current stock price
• g: Dividend growth rate

**Example:**
• D0 = $2.00, g = 5%, P0 = $40
• D1 = $2.00 × 1.05 = $2.10
• rE = ($2.10 / $40) + 5% = 5.25% + 5% = 10.25%

**Bond-Yield-Plus-Risk-Premium:**
rE = rD + Risk premium (typically 3-5%)

**Build-Up Method:**
rE = Rf + Industry premium + Size premium + Company premium

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Market vs Book Weights
sections.content:
**Use MARKET VALUES for weights!**

**Why Market, Not Book?**
• WACC reflects current financing costs
• Market values = current value
• Book values may be outdated

**Example:**
• Book equity: $100M
• Market cap: $200M
• Debt: $50M (market ≈ book)

**Correct weights:**
• Equity: $200M / $250M = 80%
• Debt: $50M / $250M = 20%

**Common Errors:**
• Using book equity weight
• Forgetting after-tax cost of debt
• Including short-term debt in permanent capital
• Using coupon rate instead of yield

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: WACC = Wd×rD(1-T) + We×rE
Cost of debt = After-tax (Interest × (1-T))
CAPM: rE = Rf + β(Rm - Rf)
Dividend growth: rE = D1/P0 + g
Use market value weights, not book
Beta > 1 = more volatile than market
WACC is minimum required return for value creation

knowledgeCheck.question: A company has 30% debt and 70% equity. Cost of debt is 6%, tax rate is 40%, and cost of equity is 12%. What is WACC?
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to use after-tax cost for debt.

title: Real Options in Capital Budgeting
description: Value flexibility in investment decisions

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Traditional NPV ignores the value of flexibility! Real options analysis recognizes that management can adapt decisions as uncertainty resolves. CMAs who understand real options can better evaluate strategic investments where flexibility has significant value.

sections.title: Limitations of Traditional NPV
sections.content:
**Traditional NPV Assumes:**
• Now-or-never decision
• Fixed path once invested
• Passive management
• All uncertainty resolved at start

**Reality:**
• Can delay investments
• Can expand if successful
• Can abandon if unsuccessful
• Uncertainty resolves over time
• Management actively adapts

**Implication:**
NPV may undervalue projects with flexibility
Strategic investments often have embedded options

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Real Options Value
sections.content:
**Expanded NPV:**

Strategic NPV = Traditional NPV + Value of Flexibility

**When Real Options Value is HIGH:**
• High uncertainty
• Long time horizons
• Significant flexibility
• Information reveals over time
• NPV near zero (close call decisions)

**When Real Options Value is LOW:**
• Little uncertainty
• Short time frame
• Limited flexibility
• Very positive or negative NPV

**Think of it like stock options:**
The right to invest (exercise) if conditions are favorable
Pay small premium now for flexibility later

sections.title: Option to Abandon Example
sections.content:
**Scenario:**
• Project NPV without abandonment: -$2M
• 50% chance high demand: NPV = +$5M
• 50% chance low demand: NPV = -$9M
• Can abandon after Year 1 for $3M salvage

**Without Option:**
NPV = 0.5($5M) + 0.5(-$9M) = -$2M → REJECT

**With Abandonment Option:**
• High demand: Continue, NPV = +$5M
• Low demand: Abandon, receive $3M (instead of -$9M)
NPV = 0.5($5M) + 0.5($3M) = $4M → ACCEPT

**Value of Abandonment Option:**
$4M - (-$2M) = $6M

The flexibility to exit transforms a bad project!

sections.title: Valuation Approaches
sections.content:
**Qualitative Assessment:**
• Identify embedded options
• Assess their significance
• Adjust judgment accordingly

**Decision Tree Analysis:**
• Map out decision points
• Assign probabilities
• Calculate expected values
• Work backward (fold back)

**Option Pricing Models:**
• Black-Scholes adaptation
• Binomial model
• More theoretically rigorous
• Requires more inputs

**CMA Level:**
Conceptual understanding + decision trees
Not complex option pricing math

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: When Options Matter Most
sections.content:
**High Option Value Situations:**

**Uncertainty:**
• Technology projects
• New markets
• R&D investments
• Natural resources

**Strategic Investments:**
• Platform investments
• Market entry
• Partnerships/JVs

**Low Option Value:**
• Routine replacements
• Cost reduction projects
• Short payback

**Exam Focus:**
• Conceptual understanding
• Types of options
• When flexibility adds value
• Simple decision tree calculations
• NOT complex Black-Scholes math

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Traditional NPV ignores management flexibility
Strategic NPV = Traditional NPV + Option value
Option types: Expand, abandon, delay, contract, switch
Options most valuable with high uncertainty + long horizon
Decision trees: Map decisions, fold back expected values
Option to abandon can transform negative NPV projects
Real options justify some investments that NPV rejects

knowledgeCheck.question: An investment has negative NPV but includes flexibility to expand significantly if market conditions improve. Real options analysis suggests:
options: The expansion option may make the project worthwhile
Use higher discount rate to compensate
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Real options analysis recognizes that flexibility has value. The option to expand if conditions are favorable adds value not captured in traditional NPV. This 'strategic NPV' (NPV + option value) may be positive even when traditional NPV is negative.
knowledgeCheck.hint: How does flexibility affect project value?

title: Lease vs Buy Analysis
description: Evaluate lease and purchase alternatives

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Should we lease or buy that equipment? CMAs analyze this decision considering cash flows, taxes, and risk. Proper lease vs buy analysis can save significant money over the asset's life. This is a practical application of capital budgeting concepts.

sections.title: Lease vs Buy Overview
sections.content:
**The Decision:**
• Acquire asset through purchase (own)
• OR acquire use through lease (rent)
• Same asset, different financing

**Key Difference:**
• Buy: Pay now, own asset, claim depreciation
• Lease: Pay over time, don't own, lease payments deductible

**Analysis Approach:**
• Compare PV of all cash flows
• Choose option with lower PV cost
• Or higher NPV if analyzing differently

**Assumption:**
• Same asset, same use
• Only financing decision differs

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Analysis Framework
sections.content:
**Net Advantage to Leasing (NAL):**

NAL = PV of Lease option - PV of Buy option

If NAL > 0 → Lease
If NAL < 0 → Buy

**Buy Cash Flows:**
- Initial cost
+ PV of depreciation tax shield
- PV of after-tax maintenance
+ PV of after-tax salvage

**Lease Cash Flows:**
- PV of after-tax lease payments
- PV of any additional costs

**Discount Rate:**
Typically after-tax cost of debt
(Lease is like secured borrowing)

**After-tax payment = Payment × (1 - Tax rate)**

sections.title: Simplified Example
sections.content:
**Equipment cost: $100,000**
**5-year life, no salvage**
**Straight-line depreciation**
**Tax rate: 40%**
**Borrowing rate: 10%, after-tax 6%**
**Lease: $24,000/year**

**Buy Analysis:**
• Cost: -$100,000 at time 0
• Depreciation: $20,000/year
• Tax shield: $20,000 × 40% = $8,000/year
• PV of tax shields: $8,000 × PVIFA(6%, 5)
  = $8,000 × 4.212 = $33,696
• PV of buying: $100,000 - $33,696 = $66,304

**Lease Analysis:**
• After-tax payment: $24,000 × (1 - 0.40) = $14,400
• PV: $14,400 × 4.212 = $60,653

**NAL = $66,304 - $60,653 = $5,651**
→ Lease is better by $5,651

sections.title: Qualitative Factors
sections.content:
**Favoring Lease:**
• Obsolescence risk (transfer to lessor)
• Limited capital
• Off-balance sheet (old GAAP)
• Maintenance included
• Flexibility
• 100% financing

**Favoring Buy:**
• Build equity in asset
• Modification allowed
• Tax benefits of ownership
• Long asset life beyond lease
• Customization needs

**Note:** ASC 842 now requires most leases on balance sheet, removing the 'off-balance sheet' advantage

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Discount Rate
sections.content:
**Which Rate to Use?**

**After-Tax Cost of Debt:**
• Lease is like secured borrowing
• Similar risk profile
• Most common approach

**NOT WACC:**
• This is financing decision, not investment
• Asset acquisition already decided
• Just comparing financing methods

**Common Mistakes:**
• Using pre-tax rate
• Forgetting to tax-adjust cash flows
• Double-counting depreciation
• Ignoring salvage value

**Remember:**
• Lease payments are FULLY deductible
• Buying: Only depreciation is deductible

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Lease vs buy: Compare PV of all cash flows
NAL = PV(Lease) - PV(Buy); positive = lease better
Buy: Initial cost offset by depreciation tax shields + salvage
Lease: After-tax lease payments
Discount rate: After-tax cost of debt (not WACC)
Lease payments fully deductible; buying only depreciation
Consider qualitative factors: obsolescence, flexibility, control

knowledgeCheck.question: In a lease vs buy analysis, which discount rate is most appropriate?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The after-tax cost of debt is appropriate because leasing is essentially a form of secured borrowing. Both the lease and the purchase with debt involve similar fixed payment obligations, so the comparison should use the after-tax borrowing rate.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Leasing is like what type of financing?

title: Capital Rationing and Project Selection
description: Select optimal projects under capital constraints

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Companies rarely have unlimited capital! When investment funds are constrained, CMAs must help select the optimal mix of projects. Capital rationing skills ensure maximum value creation within budget limits.

sections.title: Capital Rationing Basics
sections.content:
**What is Capital Rationing?**
• Limited funds for capital investment
• More positive-NPV projects than money
• Must select subset of projects
• Goal: Maximize total NPV

**Why Does Rationing Occur?**
• **Soft rationing:** Internal budget limits
  - Self-imposed constraints
  - Divisional budgets
  - Management conservatism
• **Hard rationing:** External constraints
  - Cannot raise more capital
  - Credit restrictions
  - Debt covenants

sections.title: Profitability Index
sections.content:
**Profitability Index (PI):**

PI = PV of Future Cash Flows / Initial Investment

OR

PI = (NPV + Initial Investment) / Initial Investment

PI = 1 + (NPV / Initial Investment)

**Interpretation:**
• PI > 1: Accept (positive NPV)
• PI < 1: Reject (negative NPV)
• PI = 1: Break-even

**Example:**
PV of future cash flows: $150,000
Initial investment: $100,000

PI = $150,000 / $100,000 = 1.50
• $1.50 PV for each $1.00 invested
• NPV = $50,000 = $150K - $100K

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Selection Process
sections.content:
**Budget: $300,000**

**Step 1: Rank by PI (highest first)**
• D: PI = 1.33
• A: PI = 1.30
• C: PI = 1.27
• B: PI = 1.25

**Step 2: Select in Order Until Budget Exhausted**
• D: $75,000 (Remaining: $225,000) ✓
• A: $100,000 (Remaining: $125,000) ✓
• C: $150,000 exceeds $125,000 ✗
• B: $200,000 exceeds $125,000 ✗

**Selected:** D + A = $175,000
**Total NPV:** $25,000 + $30,000 = $55,000

**Key Insight:**
PI ranks efficiency (NPV per dollar invested)
Maximizes NPV for limited capital

sections.title: When PI Ranking Fails
sections.content:
**PI Ranking Limitations:**

**Indivisible Projects:**
• Can't do half a project
• May need to try combinations

**Different Project Lives:**
• PI doesn't adjust for timing
• May need equivalent annual annuity

**Mutually Exclusive Projects:**
• Must choose one or other
• Use NPV for selection

**Example of Combination Need:**
Budget: $250,000
Option 1: D + A + leave $75K = NPV $55K
Option 2: Try B alone = NPV $50K
Option 3: C + D = $225K = NPV $65K ✓

Best combo may not follow strict PI ranking

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: NPV vs PI
sections.content:
**When to Use Which:**

**No Capital Constraint:**
• Accept ALL positive NPV projects
• PI not needed - take everything
• Use NPV for mutually exclusive choice

**With Capital Constraint:**
• Rank by PI to maximize total NPV
• PI shows efficiency per dollar

**Mutually Exclusive:**
• ALWAYS use NPV, not PI
• Even with rationing
• PI can mislead on scale

**Example:**
Project A: $100 investment, $120 PV (PI = 1.20, NPV = $20)
Project B: $1,000 investment, $1,100 PV (PI = 1.10, NPV = $100)

If mutually exclusive: B wins (higher NPV)
Even though A has higher PI

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Capital rationing: Limited funds, must select projects
PI = PV of cash flows / Initial investment
PI shows efficiency - NPV per dollar invested
Rank by PI, select highest until budget exhausted
May need to evaluate combinations for optimal mix
Mutually exclusive: Use NPV not PI
Without constraints: Accept all positive NPV

knowledgeCheck.question: A company has a $500,000 capital budget. Project X requires $300,000 and has NPV of $60,000. Project Y requires $200,000 and has NPV of $50,000. If projects are independent, which should be selected?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Both projects have positive NPV and together require $500,000 ($300K + $200K), exactly matching the budget. Total NPV = $60,000 + $50,000 = $110,000. There's no capital rationing issue since both fit within budget.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Check if the budget can accommodate both projects.

title: Sensitivity and Scenario Analysis
description: Test investment decisions under uncertainty

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: The future is uncertain! CMAs use sensitivity and scenario analysis to test how investment decisions hold up under different assumptions. These techniques reveal risks and help make better decisions under uncertainty.

sections.title: Analysis Under Uncertainty
sections.content:
**The Problem:**
• NPV calculations use estimates
• Sales, costs, timing uncertain
• Single-point estimates may mislead
• Need to understand risk

**Three Approaches:**
• **Sensitivity Analysis:** Change one variable
• **Scenario Analysis:** Change multiple variables
• **Simulation:** Probabilistic modeling

**Goal:**
• Understand which variables matter most
• See range of possible outcomes
• Make more informed decisions

sections.title: Sensitivity Analysis
sections.content:
**What It Does:**
Changes ONE variable at a time
Holds all others constant
Shows impact on NPV/IRR

**Process:**
1. Build base case model
2. Identify key variables
3. Vary each variable (+/- 10%, 20%)
4. Calculate new NPV for each
5. Rank by impact

**Output:**
• Tornado diagram
• Shows most sensitive variables at top
• Longest bars = highest impact

**Example:**
10% decrease in sales volume → NPV drops 40%
10% increase in costs → NPV drops 15%
→ Volume is more critical than costs

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Scenario Analysis
sections.content:
**Scenario Analysis:**
Changes MULTIPLE variables simultaneously
Creates alternative futures

**Typical Scenarios:**
• **Best Case:** Optimistic assumptions
• **Base Case:** Most likely
• **Worst Case:** Pessimistic assumptions

**Example:**
**Best:** High sales + Low costs + Strong economy
**Worst:** Low sales + High costs + Recession

**Advantage over Sensitivity:**
• More realistic (variables correlate)
• Shows combined effects
• Better for storytelling

**Memory:**
Sensitivity = One at a time
Scenario = Whole story

sections.title: Monte Carlo Simulation
sections.content:
**What It Is:**
• Computer-based probabilistic analysis
• Runs thousands of scenarios
• Random sampling from distributions
• Shows probability distribution of outcomes

**Process:**
1. Define probability distributions for variables
2. Computer randomly samples values
3. Calculate NPV for each combination
4. Repeat 1,000+ times
5. Analyze results

**Output:**
• Distribution of possible NPVs
• Mean, median, standard deviation
• Probability of positive NPV
• Value at Risk (VaR)

**Advantage:**
Most comprehensive risk view
Captures all interactions

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: When to Use Each
sections.content:
**Choose the Right Tool:**

**Sensitivity Analysis:**
• Quick and simple
• Identify key drivers
• Early-stage analysis
• Limited time/resources

**Scenario Analysis:**
• Board presentations
• Strategic planning
• Variables are related
• Tell coherent stories

**Monte Carlo:**
• Complex projects
• Many uncertain variables
• Need probability of outcomes
• High-stakes decisions

**Limitation of All:**
Garbage in, garbage out
Quality depends on input estimates

**Exam Tip:**
Know differences - often tested

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Sensitivity: One variable at a time, find key drivers
Scenario: Multiple variables, coherent futures (best/base/worst)
Monte Carlo: Probabilistic, thousands of simulations
Tornado diagram ranks sensitivity of variables
Scenario analysis better captures correlated changes
Monte Carlo provides probability distributions
All methods depend on quality of input assumptions

knowledgeCheck.question: An analyst wants to determine which single input variable has the greatest impact on a project's NPV. Which technique is most appropriate?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Sensitivity analysis changes ONE variable at a time while holding others constant, making it ideal for identifying which single variable has the greatest impact on NPV. Scenario analysis changes multiple variables together.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Which technique varies one variable at a time?
